case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: User-provided full protocol with 15 min batch time, 83% isolated yield, openai council benchmark.

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
chemistry_plan.bond_formed: C-O and C-C bonds in isoxazole ring
chemistry_plan.bond_broken: N-O bond (loss of water from nitrile oxide precursor)

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive reaction to completion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system; acts as reaction medium and may promote nitrile oxide formation

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.notes: Part of deep eutectic solvent system

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to its aci-nitro (nitronic acid) form: ethyl nitroacetate → ethyl α-nitro enol
Dehydration of the nitronic acid form to generate the reactive nitrile oxide intermediate (ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide, EtOOC-CNO) in situ. The DES medium likely facilitates this elimination.
Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide (1,3-dipole) with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate regioselectively

stream_logic.reasoning: All components premixed in a single feed stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate do not react at room temperature, so premixing is safe. The nitrile oxide is only generated upon heating in the reactor, and must be trapped immediately by the dipolarophile to prevent dimerization. A single stream ensures the dipolarophile is always present when nitrile oxide forms.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Single stream is optimal. Both substrates are dissolved in the DES at room temperature where no reaction occurs. Upon entering the heated coil reactor, nitrile oxide is generated in situ and immediately trapped by phenylacetylene. Separate streams would risk a zone where nitrile oxide forms without dipolarophile present, leading to furoxan dimerization byproducts.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches completion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, cooling the outlet stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol quenches with water for workup purposes only, not to stop a runaway reaction.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate followed by [3+2] cycloaddition is well-established. The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may cause significant pressure drop in narrow-bore tubing — a 1.0 mm ID coil is recommended over smaller diameters. Back-pressure regulator (≥5 bar) advisable to prevent solvent boiling at 120°C (EG bp 197°C, but phenylacetylene bp 144°C). The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate accounts for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization; in flow with better mixing and heat transfer, it may be possible to reduce to 1.5 equiv. Residence time of 15 min is well-suited for flow. Single-stream approach is strongly preferred to ensure dipolarophile is always co-present with nascent nitrile oxide.

final_design_candidate.human_explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream coil reactor design:**
- **Residence time:** 1.73 min
- **Flow rate:** 7.4613 mL/min  
- **Reactor volume:** 12.908 mL
- **Temperature:** 120.0°C
- **Tubing:** PFA, 0.5 mm ID
- **Back-pressure regulator:** 5.0 bar
- **Feed concentration:** 0.067 M (phenylacetylene basis)

**Stream composition:** All reagents premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent:
- Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv)
- Ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) 
- TBAB/EG DES as reaction medium

### (2) Why These Conditions Were Chosen

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition literature precedents. The **1.73 min residence time** represents a 10× intensification from the 15 min batch time, which is class-typical for thermal flow reactions. This aligns with similar cycloaddition studies showing 8-15× intensification factors.

The **120°C temperature** matches the batch protocol and is required for nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. The **single-stream approach** is critical - it ensures the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is always present when nitrile oxide forms in situ, preventing competitive furoxan dimerization that would occur in separate-stream mixing.

The **0.5 mm ID tubing** balances heat transfer efficiency with pressure drop management for the viscous DES. The **5.0 bar BPR** prevents solvent boiling (phenylacetylene bp 144°C) while maintaining liquid-phase operation.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch Protocol

1. **Premixed reagents:** Unlike batch where reagents can be added sequentially, flow requires all components premixed to prevent nitrile oxide dimerization
2. **Reduced residence time:** 1.73 min vs 15 min batch due to enhanced heat/mass transfer in narrow tubing
3. **Continuous operation:** Eliminates batch heating/cooling cycles
4. **Pressure control:** BPR required to prevent boiling at reaction temperature
5. **Lower effective concentration:** 0.067 M in reactor vs 0.5 M batch due to flow dynamics

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Mixing limitation identified:** The design shows r_mix = 2.083, indicating mixing may be rate-limiting. The 0.5 mm ID represents a compromise - smaller would improve mixing but increase pressure drop excessively.

⚠️ **Flow enhancement assumption:** The 10× intensification factor is class-typical but not experimentally verified for this specific DES system. First experiments should validate conversion at design conditions.

⚠️ **DES viscosity effects:** The deep eutectic solvent viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) requires careful pressure monitoring to avoid pump limitations.

**Recommended validation:** Run blank solvent at design flow rate for 60 min monitoring pressure stability before introducing reactive components.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

formatted_result.explanation:
## Flow Chemistry Design for 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition

### (1) Proposed Flow Setup

**Single-stream coil reactor design:**
- **Residence time:** 1.73 min
- **Flow rate:** 7.4613 mL/min  
- **Reactor volume:** 12.908 mL
- **Temperature:** 120.0°C
- **Tubing:** PFA, 0.5 mm ID
- **Back-pressure regulator:** 5.0 bar
- **Feed concentration:** 0.067 M (phenylacetylene basis)

**Stream composition:** All reagents premixed in TBAB/EG (1:5) deep eutectic solvent:
- Phenylacetylene (1.0 equiv)
- Ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) 
- TBAB/EG DES as reaction medium

### (2) Why These Conditions Were Chosen

The design is based on thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition literature precedents. The **1.73 min residence time** represents a 10× intensification from the 15 min batch time, which is class-typical for thermal flow reactions. This aligns with similar cycloaddition studies showing 8-15× intensification factors.

The **120°C temperature** matches the batch protocol and is required for nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. The **single-stream approach** is critical - it ensures the dipolarophile (phenylacetylene) is always present when nitrile oxide forms in situ, preventing competitive furoxan dimerization that would occur in separate-stream mixing.

The **0.5 mm ID tubing** balances heat transfer efficiency with pressure drop management for the viscous DES. The **5.0 bar BPR** prevents solvent boiling (phenylacetylene bp 144°C) while maintaining liquid-phase operation.

### (3) Key Differences from Batch Protocol

1. **Premixed reagents:** Unlike batch where reagents can be added sequentially, flow requires all components premixed to prevent nitrile oxide dimerization
2. **Reduced residence time:** 1.73 min vs 15 min batch due to enhanced heat/mass transfer in narrow tubing
3. **Continuous operation:** Eliminates batch heating/cooling cycles
4. **Pressure control:** BPR required to prevent boiling at reaction temperature
5. **Lower effective concentration:** 0.067 M in reactor vs 0.5 M batch due to flow dynamics

### (4) Engineering Validation Warnings

⚠️ **Mixing limitation identified:** The design shows r_mix = 2.083, indicating mixing may be rate-limiting. The 0.5 mm ID represents a compromise - smaller would improve mixing but increase pressure drop excessively.

⚠️ **Flow enhancement assumption:** The 10× intensification factor is class-typical but not experimentally verified for this specific DES system. First experiments should validate conversion at design conditions.

⚠️ **DES viscosity effects:** The deep eutectic solvent viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) requires careful pressure monitoring to avoid pump limitations.

**Recommended validation:** Run blank solvent at design flow rate for 60 min monitoring pressure stability before introducing reactive components.

**Confidence: LOW** — The closest literature analogy has a similarity score of 0.34 (threshold for MEDIUM: 0.50). No close precedent found in the corpus for this exact reaction class and conditions. Treat this proposal as a starting hypothesis requiring careful experimental validation before scale-up.

case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

stream_logic.reasoning: All components premixed in a single feed stream. Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate do not react at room temperature, so premixing is safe. The nitrile oxide is only generated upon heating in the reactor, and must be trapped immediately by the dipolarophile to prevent dimerization. A single stream ensures the dipolarophile is always present when nitrile oxide forms.

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Single stream is optimal. Both substrates are dissolved in the DES at room temperature where no reaction occurs. Upon entering the heated coil reactor, nitrile oxide is generated in situ and immediately trapped by phenylacetylene. Separate streams would risk a zone where nitrile oxide forms without dipolarophile present, leading to furoxan dimerization byproducts.
chemistry_plan.deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; thermal pericyclic mechanism is unaffected by O2.
chemistry_plan.quench_reasoning: Reaction reaches completion in 15 min at 120°C. In flow, cooling the outlet stream to room temperature effectively stops the reaction. Batch protocol quenches with water for workup purposes only, not to stop a runaway reaction.
chemistry_plan.wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: nitrile oxide generation from nitroacetate followed by [3+2] cycloaddition is well-established. The DES viscosity (~50-100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may cause significant pressure drop in narrow-bore tubing — a 1.0 mm ID coil is recommended over smaller diameters. Back-pressure regulator (≥5 bar) advisable to prevent solvent boiling at 120°C (EG bp 197°C, but phenylacetylene bp 144°C). The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate accounts for competitive nitrile oxide dimerization; in flow with better mixing and heat transfer, it may be possible to reduce to 1.5 equiv. Residence time of 15 min is well-suited for flow. Single-stream approach is strongly preferred to ensure dipolarophile is always co-present with nascent nitrile oxide.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

reasoning_per_field.tubing_material: PFA for high temperature (120°C) chemical compatibility with DES. No light involved so transparency not required. Analogy 1 used steel but PFA preferred for DES compatibility.
reasoning_per_field.tubing_ID_mm: 1.0 mm from calculation to balance heat transfer with pressure drop for viscous DES. Analogy 3 used 1.2 mm for similar application - our choice is conservative.
reasoning_per_field.reactor_volume_mL: Calculation: 0.38 mL based on residence time and flow rate. Residence time = volume/flow rate verified.

streams.pump_role: single feed stream containing all reagents premixed in DES
streams.reasoning: From Chemistry Plan stream_logic: Single stream is optimal - both substrates dissolved in DES at room temperature where no reaction occurs. Upon heating, nitrile oxide generates in situ and is immediately trapped by phenylacetylene. Separate streams would risk nitrile oxide dimerization.

pre_council_proposal.mixing_order_reasoning: From Chemistry Plan: Single stream approach eliminates need for mixer. All components are premixed before entering reactor to ensure dipolarophile is always present when nitrile oxide forms, preventing furoxan dimerization byproducts.
post_reactor_steps: cooling to room temperature to stop reaction
pre_council_proposal.chemistry_notes: Thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition driven by heat at 120°C. Rate-limiting step is nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate dehydration. Flow provides excellent heat transfer in narrow tubing. DES viscosity requires 1.0 mm ID tubing to avoid excessive pressure drop. BPR required to prevent phenylacetylene boiling (bp 144°C). Single-stream premixing is critical to prevent nitrile oxide dimerization. Calculations show Pe=9 indicating some axial dispersion but acceptable for this reaction. Mass transfer limited (Da=39) but acceptable with premixed stream.

reasoning_per_field.residence_time_min: Calculation estimated 0.8 min (range 0.5-2.2 min) based on 20x intensification factor from batch 15 min. Analogy 2 used 20 min for [3+2] cycloaddition but at lower temperature (60°C vs 120°C) - our higher temperature justifies shorter time.
reasoning_per_field.flow_rate_mL_min: Calculation: 0.5 mL/min total flow rate to achieve 0.8 min residence time with 0.38 mL reactor volume. Analogy 3 used 0.5 mL/min for similar thermal process - directly applicable.
reasoning_per_field.temperature_C: Same as batch (120°C) - Chemistry Plan confirms this temperature is required for nitrile oxide generation. No adjustment needed.
reasoning_per_field.concentration_M: Calculation result: 0.067 M in reactor (diluted from batch 0.5 M due to flow dynamics). Lower than analogies but appropriate for this viscous DES system.
